proven.claim: PROVEN: The log at line 40 fires BEFORE the mutation at line 41,
proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: [NEW] — State machine constraint
; LINE: 40
; PROVEN: The log at line 40 fires BEFORE the mutation at line 41,
; so logged_state accurately reflects the pre-mutation state.
; The "was in state ${order.state}" portion is always truthful.
(declare-const pre_state Int)
(declare-const logged_state Int)
; Code transition: log executes at line 40, mutation at line 41 — order matters
; Therefore: logged_state captures order.state at function entry = pre_state
(assert (= logged_state pre_state))
; Negate: can logged_state differ from pre_state?
(assert (not (= logged_state pre_state)))
(check-sat)

violations.claim: The log says "Order X cancelled: reason, was in state Y".
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: [NEW] — State machine constraint
; LINE: 40
; The log says "Order X cancelled: reason, was in state Y".
; The word "cancelled" implies a valid cancellation is being performed.
; But the log fires unconditionally — even when the state machine forbids the transition.
; This makes the log's claim ("cancelled") semantically false for shipped(3) and cancelled(4).
(declare-const pre_state Int)
(declare-const logged_state Int)
(declare-const is_valid_source Int) ; 1 if pre_state ∈ {0,1,2}, 0 otherwise
; Code transition: log fires first, captures state before mutation
(assert (= logged_state pre_state))
; Code transition: valid sources are draft(0), submitted(1), approved(2)
; shipped(3) is NOT a valid source per VALID_TRANSITIONS
(assert (= pre_state 3))
; Violation: log claims "cancelled" but VALID_TRANSITIONS[3] = [] forbids this
; The log's implicit validity claim is false
(assert (not (or (= logged_state 0) (= logged_state 1) (= logged_state 2))))
(check-sat)

(assert (= pre_state 3))
